children.name: Echinus
children.description: A genus of globular sea urchins with a hemispherical test covered in sharp spines. Includes the European edible sea urchin, one of the most familiar echinoderms of the northeastern Atlantic.

children.name: Echinus esculentus
children.description: A large, spiny sea urchin found on rocky substrates of the northeastern Atlantic from Norway to Portugal. Its gonads (roe) are a prized delicacy — particularly in French, Japanese, and Mediterranean cuisines.

children.name: Echinus melo
children.description: Distinguished by large, widely spaced primary spines and a melon-shaped test. Found in the Mediterranean and adjacent Atlantic at depths of 20-100 metres.

children.name: Strongylocentrotus
children.description: A genus of cold-water sea urchins from the northern Pacific and Atlantic. They are important herbivores in kelp forest ecosystems and can cause kelp deforestation at high densities.

children.name: Strongylocentrotus droebachiensis
children.description: A circumpolar Arctic and subarctic species common in the North Atlantic and North Pacific. Feeds on kelp and algae; population irruptions have caused extensive destruction of kelp forests along the Norwegian and Canadian coasts.

children.name: Strongylocentrotus purpuratus
children.description: A model organism in developmental and molecular biology, the first echinoderm to have its genome sequenced. Native to the Pacific coast of North America from Alaska to Baja California.

children.name: Strongylocentrotus franciscanus
children.description: The largest species of sea urchin in the Pacific, reaching test diameters over 15 cm. Commercially harvested for its large gonads. Individuals can live over 100 years.

children.name: Psammechinus
children.description: A genus of small, shallow-water sea urchins with a flattened test and short spines. Common in the northeastern Atlantic and Mediterranean.

children.name: Psammechinus miliaris
children.description: A small, greenish urchin found from Iceland to Morocco in the shallow subtidal and intertidal zones. Its roe is harvested and sold alongside that of Echinus esculentus.

children.name: Psammechinus microtuberculatus
children.description: Common in the Mediterranean Sea on seagrass beds and rocky substrates. Distinguished by its small tubercles and pale greenish colouration with white spine tips.

children.name: Paracentrotus
children.description: A genus of sea urchins from the eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean, highly valued for their edible gonads. Paracentrotus lividus is one of the most commercially important sea urchin species.

children.name: Paracentrotus lividus
children.description: The most commercially harvested sea urchin in Europe, collected for its roe (uni) especially in France, Spain, and Italy. Found in the Mediterranean and the eastern Atlantic from Scotland to Morocco.
